In the mutant, line 89 changes from checking (hoursOffset == 0 && minutesOffset == 0) to (false && minutesOffset == 0). So instead of returning UTC when both are zero, it never returns UTC because false makes the condition always false. Also, the check for minutes being within 0-59 is moved up before that.

This change would cause the method to skip returning UTC even when both offsets are zero and then throw an exception if minutesOffset isn't valid. But wait, in the original code, it only throws if minutesOffset is out of range, regardless of hours. So with the mutant, for cases where hoursOffset is non-zero but minutesOffset is invalid, the same check applies.

The key here is whether this mutant introduces a new bug or just changes some existing logic without causing adjacent faults. Since other tests are still failing, it suggests that the mutant's code might be related to those issues but isn't directly fixing them.

So, considering if the mutant is adjacent to a fault: The mutation didn't fix all failing tests, only one. The others still fail because their underlying issues aren't addressed by this change. Therefore, the mutant doesn't resolve adjacent faults for all test cases.
